name: Callanish Stones
description: The Callanish Stones, also known as Calanais Standing Stones, are a collection of standing stones erected in a cruciform pattern on the Isle of Lewis in the Outer Hebrides of Scotland. Dating back to the Neolithic era (around 3000 BC), their purpose remains shrouded in mystery, fueling numerous myths and legends. The most prominent theory suggests they were used for astronomical observations, aligning with the movements of the sun and moon. However, local folklore paints a more mystical picture. One legend claims the stones are petrified giants who refused to convert to Christianity. Another tale describes them as a gateway to the otherworld, a place where mortals could interact with spirits and deities. The stones are believed to hold powerful energies, influencing the landscape and the lives of those who live near them. Their placement and alignment suggest a deep understanding of celestial events and a connection to ancient spiritual practices. The central stone, taller than the rest, serves as a focal point for these energies, and the surrounding landscape echoes the spiritual significance of the site. The Callanish Stones continue to inspire awe and wonder, reminding us of the enduring power of myth and the enduring mystery of our ancestors.
subtitle: Ancient stones, mystical gateway

keyMyths.title: Petrified Giants
keyMyths.description: Legend claims the stones are giants turned to stone for refusing Christianity. This highlights the clash between pagan beliefs and the arrival of Christianity.

keyMyths.title: Gateway to the Otherworld
keyMyths.description: The stones are considered a portal to the spirit world, allowing interaction with spirits. This emphasizes the liminal nature of the site.

sources.author: Aubrey Burl
sources.work: The Stone Circles of Britain, Ireland and Brittany
sources.description: Comprehensive study of stone circles including Callanish.
sources.text: Comprehensive study of stone circles including Callanish.

sources.author: Margaret Curtis
sources.description: Discusses the broader cultural context of standing stones in Scotland.
